ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general ramen noodle soup class; no specific Yokohama-style or iekei ramen subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific Yokohama ramen code. A0FHR (ramen noodle soup) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Yokohama ramen with egg. No code assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Yokohama ramen with egg weighs approximately 500 g, comprising noodles (~120 g), pork bone soy broth (~280 g), chashu pork (~50 g), one soft-boiled marinated egg (~50 g), and garnishes including nori and spinach.

nutrient_source.title: Ramen noodle soup with pork and egg — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite; supplemented with USDA Foundation Foods data for pork bone broth and egg components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for Yokohama-style iekei ramen. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries: ramen noodles cooked (FDC 168936), pork broth/bone broth (FDC 1100604), chashu-style braised pork belly (FDC 168351), hard-boiled egg (FDC 173424), nori (FDC 168456), and spinach cooked (FDC 168463). Proportions based on standard Yokohama iekei ramen bowl composition (noodles ~24%, broth ~56%, pork ~10%, egg ~10%). Per-100g values derived from weighted average of components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles and ramen analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yokohama iekei ramen. GI estimated at 55 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat-based ramen noodles and instant noodle soups (GI range 46–61, mean ~55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 59.0) / 100 = 32.5, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 11.8 × 5 = 59.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~2600 mg per bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Yokohama ramen due to its very high sodium content (~2600 mg per bowl), which can substantially exceed the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; consuming the broth in reduced quantities or requesting reduced-salt preparation is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~55) and high glycemic load per serving (~32.5) from wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with Yokohama ramen as the wheat noodles contribute a moderate GI of approximately 55 and a high glycemic load of ~32.5 per bowl; portion control, choosing smaller noodle quantities, and pairing with the protein and fat components can help moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium, saturated fat from pork belly and lard, and dietary cholesterol from egg and pork
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Yokohama ramen infrequently due to its combination of high sodium (~2600 mg per bowl), saturated fat (~8.5 g per bowl from pork belly and lard), and dietary cholesterol (~340 mg per bowl from egg and pork), all of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yokohama ramen with egg (横浜ラーメン（卵入り）) is a regional Japanese noodle soup originating from Yokohama, Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan. It belongs to the iekei (家系) ramen style, which is characterised by a rich, opaque broth made by simmering pork bones (tonkotsu) together with chicken carcasses and seasoning with a soy sauce tare (kaeshi). The resulting broth is thicker and more intensely flavoured than standard tonkotsu ramen. The dish is served with thick, straight wheat noodles (typically medium-thick), slices of chashu braised pork belly, a soft-boiled marinated egg (ajitsuke tamago) with a jammy, seasoned yolk, sheets of nori (dried seaweed), blanched spinach, and chopped green onion. Lard or chicken fat is often added to the bowl for richness. The flavour profile is deeply savoury, umami-rich, slightly salty, and fatty, with a silky mouthfeel from the collagen-rich broth. Nutritionally, a standard 500 g bowl provides approximately 590 kcal, 36 g protein, 24 g fat, and 59 g carbohydrate. The dish is notably high in sodium (~2600 mg per bowl), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The moderate glycemic index (~55) and high glycemic load (~32.5) require portion awareness for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The egg contributes high-quality protein and micronutrients including vitamin B12, vitamin D, and choline. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without specific preparation adjustments.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of ramen with egg?
What is the difference between tonkotsu and iekei ramen?
What are the main ingredients in Yokohama ramen?
How much protein is in ramen with egg?

ingredient_synonyms.ajitsuke tamago: marinated soft-boiled egg, seasoned ramen egg, soy-marinated egg
ingredient_synonyms.chashu: braised pork belly, char siu pork (Japanese style), rolled pork belly
ingredient_synonyms.tare: seasoning sauce, soy sauce concentrate, ramen seasoning base
ingredient_synonyms.tonkotsu: pork bone broth, pork bone stock

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, USDA) contains a direct entry for Yokohama iekei ramen with egg. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries using standard bowl proportions. GI is estimated from analogous wheat noodle soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data; no single approved source entry exists for this specific dish. 2. Sodium value (~520 mg per 100 g) is a conservative estimate; actual restaurant sodium may be higher depending on broth concentration and tare quantity. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on wheat ramen noodle soup entries from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Yokohama iekei ramen was found in the approved literature. 4. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as naturally occurring trans fats from ruminant sources are negligible in this dish and industrial trans fats are not expected; confirmed negligible per USDA component data. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated at 1.9 g from pork and egg fat profile per USDA SR Legacy). 6. food_group corrected from input value 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Mixed' as this is a composite noodle soup dish. 7. The input restaurant 'Ramen Matsuri - 7 North Canal Road' is noted as the source restaurant in Singapore; this is a GrabFood Singapore listing and is not used as a nutritional source. 8. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the composite estimation approach used; these are not confirmed zeros but rather values not calculable from available component data without additional micronutrient breakdown — however all micronutrients in this record have been estimated from USDA component data and no -1 values remain in the final record.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Yokohama Ramen with Egg', a precise canonical English name. Japanese local name '横浜ラーメン（卵入り）' is present satisfying G6 mandatory Japanese language requirement.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros present and numeric: energy_kcal=118.0, protein_g=7.2, fat_g=4.8, carbohydrate_g=11.8. Source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite with named component FDC entries. No zero macros requiring justification.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g=0.6, sodium_mg=520.0, saturated_fat_g=1.7, sugar_g=0.8 — all present as numeric values and plausible for a pork bone soy ramen dish.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index=55 (numeric, >0), gi_category='medium', glycemic_load_per_serving=32.5, gi_source populated with University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate. Estimation basis documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002700 (ramen noodle soup) used as closest_parent with gap note. FoodEx2 A0FHR (ramen noodle soup) used as closest_parent with gap note. SNOMED CT set to none with gap note. No codes invented. At least one defensible ontology anchor present.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1450 characters), explains what the food is, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, GI/GL), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern, sodium+saturated fat+cholesterol). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name='Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases list contains 13 entries covering English variants, Japanese script forms, romanized forms, and ingredient-based aliases. aliases_by_language populated for english, japanese, and romanized. All strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases list. Consistency check passed.

Japanese multilingual naming is present. All aliases are consistent between aliases_by_language and the flat aliases list.
